video_title: Quantum 101 (with Chris Miller and Zach Yerushalmi)
gist: Elevate Quantum CEO Zach Yerushalmi and Chris Miller frame quantum computing as the highest-stakes industrial race since the atomic bomb, where the US moat over China is 'freakishly small' (unlike semiconductors). Industry timelines for a useful/cryptography-breaking machine compressed from 10+ years to 3-5 years over the past year, making post-quantum encryption migration the most urgent and economically immediate threat, while talent (3 jobs per 1 qualified candidate) — not money — is the binding constraint.

insights.claim: Unlike semiconductors where the US holds a decades-long moat over China, the US lead in quantum computing is 'freakishly small,' raising the stakes of getting policy right from the outset.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Investors should treat quantum as a fast-moving, winner-take-share national-security race where US incumbency offers far less protection than in chips — execution and timing matter more.
insights.quote: in semicon we have literally decades of moat over China... in quantum I think our margin of error is freakishly small

insights.claim: Yerushalmi argues quantum is the single biggest lever society can pull over the next couple of decades and carries more at stake than any industrial program since the atomic bomb.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Frames quantum as a top-tier macro/geopolitical theme worth tracking despite its current small market size.
insights.quote: we uh have more at stake here than any industrial program since the atomic bomb

insights.claim: Winning the quantum race is defined as building the first commercially useful ('fault tolerant') system first AND maintaining or retaining access to the best capability long-term, analogous to building the first useful vacuum-tube computer.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Sets a two-part scorecard (first-mover + durable lead) for evaluating which nations and companies are actually 'winning' beyond hype releases.

themes.theme: Decryption as the immediate, threshold threat

insights.claim: Industry timelines for a cryptographically capable quantum computer compressed over the past year from '10-plus years out' to a 3-5 year horizon (~2030), driven by recent engineering breakthroughs.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: A 3-5 year clock on code-breaking compresses the window for cybersecurity, financial-infrastructure, and crypto-asset risk repricing far sooner than consensus assumes.
insights.quote: Last year that went from, you know, 10 plus years out to with these breakthroughs it's on a you know, three to five year basis

insights.claim: Quantum computers threaten all current encryption via Shor's algorithm by efficiently solving the 'hidden subgroup problem' — specifically factoring large primes (RSA) and elliptical curve cryptography, the latter securing Bitcoin signatures.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Directly flags Bitcoin and RSA/ECC-secured assets as exposed to quantum decryption risk — a tail risk for crypto and financial infrastructure.
insights.quote: all of our financial infrastructure and things like Bitcoin are underpinned by this problem that a classical computer literally takes age of the universe to solve and a quantum computer looks at, laughs at, and... steals your Bitcoin wallet

insights.claim: The NIST post-quantum migration mandate (all government systems by 2028-2029, consumer by 2035) is dangerously slow because the threat may arrive ~2030, compressing the normal 10-15 year security upgrade cycle into 3-5 years.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: A mismatch between threat timeline and migration timeline creates a multi-year window of systemic vulnerability — bullish for post-quantum security vendors, bearish for laggard institutions.
insights.quote: typically you want 10 to 15 years as an upgrade cycle... and we have three to five

insights.claim: The recommended replacement standard, NIST lattice-based encryption, has mature theory but immature implementation that hasn't been deployed at real scale, compounding migration risk.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Signals execution risk in the post-quantum transition itself — the 'fix' is unproven at scale, leaving room for further security incidents and vendor opportunity.
insights.quote: the one that we're moving to... it just really hasn't been deployed at any real scale

insights.claim: Unlike AI whose payoffs accrue gradually, decryption has an immediate 'threshold' payoff once achieved, creating game-theoretic pressure for the US or China to divert all quantum resources Defense-Production-Act style if six months from a breakthrough.
insights.speaker: host (Jordan Schneider)
insights.so_what: The binary, immediate-payoff nature of decryption changes state behavior and escalation risk relative to AI — a distinct geopolitical scenario to model.
insights.quote: if you're 6 months away from decryption, like, you know, at what point do you just say all quantum computing resources must be devoted to this task? Um defense production act style

insights.claim: The first code-breaking quantum computer will be inefficient — taking a month or many months per code — forcing operators to 'choose their bullets very assiduously' on highest-value targets like Chinese nuclear codes.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Tempers the 'instant collapse of all encryption' narrative — early decryption capability is scarce and rationed, so impact is targeted, not blanket, at first.
insights.quote: it could take like a month or many months in order to break one code. Um which means you got to choose your bullets very very uh assiduously

insights.claim: Accurate corrosion modeling — not drug discovery — may be the first major quantum application, worth tens of billions to global shipping and national security because it is a simpler, more tractable problem.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Redirects attention from the consensus drug-discovery TAM toward corrosion/materials as the nearer-term commercial wedge for quantum revenue.
insights.quote: the first would actually maybe not even be life sciences. It would be something around corrosion... worth tens of billions of dollars to the global shipping and national security sector

insights.claim: Quantum's core molecular-modeling advantage stems from exponential state-space scaling (2^n) that defeats classical computers — modeling penicillin's 42 atoms classically would require ~10^86 transistors, more than atoms in the observable universe.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Quantifies why quantum is the only path to rational molecular design — grounding the materials/drug-discovery thesis in a hard physical limit classical compute and AI cannot overcome.
insights.quote: 10 to the 86 would be needing more transistors than there are atoms in the observable universe

insights.claim: Roughly 186 qubits could model penicillin, and because the systems scale exponentially, going from 1,000-qubit systems enables rational design of much more complex molecular systems worth hundreds of billions to trillions of dollars.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Establishes a concrete qubit-count roadmap (hundreds to thousands) for unlocking high-value materials/pharma markets — a milestone to benchmark hardware progress against.
insights.quote: you need something like 186 of these quantum bits, qubits. Which we're not there yet, but we're more or less on the cusp of

insights.claim: Yerushalmi contrasts quantum with AI: AI is 'really really good fancy curve fitting' that guesses, while quantum solves problems 'ab initio' from first principles and locks in correct solutions.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Differentiates quantum's value proposition from AI for investors conflating the two — quantum targets problems AI structurally cannot solve, implying complementary not competing TAMs.
insights.quote: AI is a lot like stabbing the dark... It's still really really really good fancy curve fitting. Quantum computers are... not guessing. It's ab initio.

insights.claim: The most strategically dangerous quantum use may be recursive material-science design — a system that rationally designs high-temperature superconductors or nuclear chemistry could lock down IP and know-how to block adversaries.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Points to a durable, compounding moat beyond hardware: whoever designs breakthrough materials first can fence off IP space — a strategic-asset angle for materials and defense.
insights.quote: you would use that and lock down IP space and know-how in a way that would block out... adversaries and competitors

insights.claim: Western governments committed roughly $23 billion to quantum over the last three years, motivated mostly by encryption fears rather than molecular-modeling upside.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the public-funding tailwind and reveals defense/security as the dominant demand driver — relevant for sizing government-contract revenue for quantum firms.
insights.quote: global Western governments... committed something like 23 billion dollars to quantum in the last 3 years

insights.claim: Big Tech quantum programs (Microsoft, Google) are 'rounding errors' on their balance sheets — a few billion a year spent mainly to avoid a Sputnik moment rather than as core capex bets.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Suggests incumbent quantum spend is defensive insurance, not conviction — so pure-play quantum exposure may sit more in startups and government programs than in mega-cap balance sheets.
insights.quote: relative to everything else they're doing, it's rounding errors on their balance sheet

insights.claim: Superconducting qubits (the modality John Martinis won a recent Nobel for) captured 60-80% of industry investment to date, but neutral-atom approaches — dismissed as a joke 3-5 years ago — are now a leading contender.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Modality risk is real — capital concentrated in superconducting could be disrupted by neutral atoms, so investors should track which qubit approach scales rather than betting on the incumbent technology.
insights.quote: superconducting... That's gotten 60 to 80% of the investment to date for the industry. But there are newer approaches. The most prominent of them is probably neutral atoms.

insights.claim: Google, despite pioneering superconducting qubits, gave neutral-atom startup QuEra roughly $250 million — illustrating incumbents hedging across modalities by investing in disruptive startups.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: A concrete signal that incumbents see neutral atoms as a serious threat — strategic-investment and M&A flow into neutral-atom startups is a leading indicator of modality momentum.
insights.quote: they're so worried and interested in neutral atoms, they just gave QuEra $250

insights.claim: IBM runs the flagship quantum program with billions invested, but quantum follows the biotech pattern where disruptive newer approaches are pioneered by startups that big players then invest in or acquire.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Frames quantum's value chain as biotech-like: startups source disruptive innovation and exits come via incumbent acquisition — guiding where venture returns concentrate.
insights.quote: IBM is um It is the flagship program in quantum

themes.theme: Policy, institutions & the biotech mental model

insights.claim: Yerushalmi's core mental model treats quantum like biotech — distinguishing 'drug' modalities (qubit types) from 'tools,' with the industry now at 'phase one clinical trials,' requiring biotech-style commercial staging.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Offers a reusable lens for valuing quantum firms via clinical-trial-style milestone risk and staged payouts rather than traditional tech metrics.
insights.quote: the mental model that I always use for quantum is biotech

insights.claim: DARPA's Quantum Benchmarking Initiative — the largest public program in the agency's history, spending ~$600 million this year — uses an advanced-market-commitment prize structure mirroring biotech clinical phases.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: A concrete, large government demand-pull program with staged prizes — its milestone payouts are a roadmap for which quantum companies attract follow-on private capital.
insights.quote: I think this year alone they're looking to spend $600 million on that

insights.claim: DARPA's QBI offers staged payouts — ~$1M table stakes, $15-20M at a scientific threshold, then $300M for a credible demonstration-scale system — explicitly sized large enough to pull venture investors into the private market.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: These prize tiers function as de-risking milestones; firms clearing phase three signal near-'FDA approval' status likely to win government procurement and venture follow-on.
insights.quote: you can get paid 300 million dollars if your system is deemed credible to that stage and you build a demonstration scale capability

insights.claim: The biggest missing institution is an IMEC-style public-private nonprofit specializing in the intermediate TRL phase between fundamental R&D and market — the pre-competitive prototyping gap that is too expensive for startups.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural bottleneck (prototyping infrastructure) where policy/capital could unlock the field — a gap that shared-fab/tooling ventures could fill.
insights.quote: If I could create one institution, it would be an IMEC

insights.claim: Elevate Quantum is the US government's first and only major place-based quantum investment, anchored in the Mountain West cluster that holds roughly half of US quantum jobs and deployed capital.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Geographic concentration of quantum talent and capital in the Mountain West identifies where the cluster economics and government dollars flow.
insights.quote: it's probably close to half of US quantum jobs, half of deployed capital

insights.claim: Talent — not money — is the true bottleneck: you could spend a trillion dollars on quantum but humans are needed to wire refrigerators and optics tables, so capital can be 'overfed' without reaching the goal.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Reframes the investment thesis: throwing capital at quantum has sharply diminishing returns past the talent constraint, so talent-rich firms/clusters are the scarce asset.
insights.quote: The bottleneck is really around talent. And so, that's where the prioritization of where you're spending the money, to my mind, is everything.

insights.claim: There are roughly three quantum job openings for every one qualified candidate, and the supply is hard to fix because a quantum PhD takes 5-7 years — a timeline that cannot be compressed even with unlimited money.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: A structural multi-year talent shortage caps how fast the industry can scale — a hard ceiling on growth and a driver of escalating researcher compensation.
insights.quote: for every three quantum posted jobs, you only get one qualified candidate

insights.claim: Because quantum hardware is too expensive for student training, graduates are theory-trained with no hands-on system access and must be retrained from scratch by employers — making quantum system engineers and technicians the priority roles to expand.
insights.speaker: Zach Yerushalmi (CEO, Elevate Quantum)
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, addressable training gap (physical-system access for technicians/engineers) — a niche for training infrastructure and a hiring-cost headwind for quantum firms.
insights.quote: when you graduate, you get hired by a company and you kind of have to get trained from scratch because you haven't had access to the physical system in the first place
